title: Ceuta

infobox.value: 0.860[[5]](#cite_note-GlobalDataLab-5) very high · [19th](/wiki/List_of_Spanish_autonomous_communities_by_Human_Development_Index "List of Spanish autonomous communities by Human Development Index")

([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ˈsjuːtə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˈseɪuːtə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Spanish: [[ˈθewta, ˈsewta]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pronunciation_of_Ceuta_in_Spanish.ogg "File:Pronunciation of Ceuta in Spanish.ogg"); [Moroccan Arabic](/wiki/Moroccan_Arabic_language "Moroccan Arabic language"): سَبْتَة, romanized: *Sabtah*) is an [autonomous city](/wiki/Autonomous_communities_of_Spain#Autonomous_cities "Autonomous communities of Spain") of [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") on the [North African](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") coast. Bordered by Morocco, it lies along the boundary between the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") and the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). Ceuta is one of the [special member state territories of the European Union](/wiki/Special_member_state_territories_and_the_European_Union "Special member state territories and the European Union").

has been said to have been a [Punic](/wiki/Punic_language "Punic language") name ("Lofty Mountain"[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECauvin_&_al.1843-11) or "Mountain of [God](/wiki/El_(deity) "El (deity)")") for [Jebel Musa](/wiki/Jebel_Musa_(Morocco) "Jebel Musa (Morocco)"),[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBonney_&_al.190726-12) the southern [Pillar of Hercules](/wiki/Pillars_of_Hercules "Pillars of Hercules").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1854-13) The name of the mountain was in fact *Habenna* ([Punic](/wiki/Punic_language "Punic language"): 𐤀𐤁‬𐤍‬, *ʾbn*, "Stone" or "[Stele](/wiki/Stele "Stele")") or *ʾAbin-ḥīq* (𐤀𐤁‬𐤍𐤇‬𐤒, *ʾbnḥq*, "Rock of the Bay"), about the nearby [Bay of Benzú](/wiki/Benz%C3%BA "Benzú").[[14]](#cite_note-lip-14) The name was [hellenized](/wiki/Hellenization "Hellenization") variously as *Ápini* ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Ἄπινι),[[14]](#cite_note-lip-14) *Abýla* (Ἀβύλα), *Abýlē* (Ἀβύλη), *Ablýx* (Ἀβλύξ), and *Abilē Stḗlē* (Ἀβίλη Στήλη, "Pillar of Abyla")[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1854-13) and in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") as **Abyla Mons** ("Mount Abyla") or **Abyla Columna** ("the Pillar of Abyla").

**Ceuta** ([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ˈsjuːtə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˈseɪuːtə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Spanish: [[ˈθewta, ˈsewta]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pronunciation_of_Ceuta_in_Spanish.ogg "File:Pronunciation of Ceuta in Spanish.ogg"); [Moroccan Arabic](/wiki/Moroccan_Arabic_language "Moroccan Arabic language"): سَبْتَة, romanized: *Sabtah*) is an [autonomous city](/wiki/Autonomous_communities_of_Spain#Autonomous_cities "Autonomous communities of Spain") of [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") on the [North African](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") coast. Bordered by Morocco, it lies along the boundary between the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") and the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). Ceuta is one of the [special member state territories of the European Union](/wiki/Special_member_state_territories_and_the_European_Union "Special member state territories and the European Union").

[Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") founded a settlement in the [peninsula of Almina](/wiki/Peninsula_of_Almina "Peninsula of Almina"), which had continuity under Roman and Byzantine rule (*Septem Fratres*). It was annexed to the early Arab Caliphates upon the [Islamic conquest of the Maghreb](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Maghreb "Muslim conquest of the Maghreb"), only to be destroyed during the [Berber Revolt](/wiki/Berber_Revolt "Berber Revolt"). It was rebuilt in the 9th century by Majkasa [Ghomaras](/wiki/Ghomaras "Ghomaras"), and for much of the middle ages, regional powers north and south of the [Strait of Gibraltar](/wiki/Strait_of_Gibraltar "Strait of Gibraltar") vied for control over Ceuta, which was a key contested port in the so-called [Battle of the Strait](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Strait "Battle of the Strait"). In 1415, it was annexed to the [Kingdom of Portugal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal"), and subsequently, after 1580, to the [Hispanic Monarchy](/wiki/Hispanic_Monarchy_(political_entity) "Hispanic Monarchy (political entity)"), with the city choosing to stay in the latter after 1640. Ceuta was a regular [municipality](/wiki/Municipalities_in_Spain "Municipalities in Spain") belonging to the Spanish [province of Cádiz](/wiki/Province_of_C%C3%A1diz "Province of Cádiz") prior to the passing of its [Statute of Autonomy](/wiki/Statute_of_Autonomy "Statute of Autonomy") in March 1995,[[8]](#cite_note-8) as provided by the Spanish Constitution, henceforth becoming an autonomous city.

Ceuta, like [Melilla](/wiki/Melilla "Melilla") and the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands"), was classified as a [free port](/wiki/Free_port "Free port") before Spain joined the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Its population is predominantly [Christian](/wiki/Christian "Christian") and [Muslim](/wiki/Islam_in_Spain "Islam in Spain"), with a small minority of [Sephardic Jews](/wiki/Sephardic_Jews "Sephardic Jews") and [Sindhi Hindus](/wiki/Sindhi_Hindus "Sindhi Hindus"), from Pakistan.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") is the official language, while [Darija Arabic](/wiki/Moroccan_Arabic "Moroccan Arabic") is also widely spoken.

## Names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Names")]

The name **Abyla** has been said to have been a [Punic](/wiki/Punic_language "Punic language") name ("Lofty Mountain"[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECauvin_&_al.1843-11) or "Mountain of [God](/wiki/El_(deity) "El (deity)")") for [Jebel Musa](/wiki/Jebel_Musa_(Morocco) "Jebel Musa (Morocco)"),[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBonney_&_al.190726-12) the southern [Pillar of Hercules](/wiki/Pillars_of_Hercules "Pillars of Hercules").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1854-13) The name of the mountain was in fact *Habenna* ([Punic](/wiki/Punic_language "Punic language"): 𐤀𐤁‬𐤍‬, *ʾbn*, "Stone" or "[Stele](/wiki/Stele "Stele")") or *ʾAbin-ḥīq* (𐤀𐤁‬𐤍𐤇‬𐤒, *ʾbnḥq*, "Rock of the Bay"), about the nearby [Bay of Benzú](/wiki/Benz%C3%BA "Benzú").[[14]](#cite_note-lip-14) The name was [hellenized](/wiki/Hellenization "Hellenization") variously as *Ápini* ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Ἄπινι),[[14]](#cite_note-lip-14) *Abýla* (Ἀβύλα), *Abýlē* (Ἀβύλη), *Ablýx* (Ἀβλύξ), and *Abilē Stḗlē* (Ἀβίλη Στήλη, "Pillar of Abyla")[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1854-13) and in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") as **Abyla Mons** ("Mount Abyla") or **Abyla Columna** ("the Pillar of Abyla").

The settlement below Jebel Musa was later renamed for the seven hills around the site, collectively referred to as the "Seven Brothers"[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmedley_&_al.184549-15) ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Ἑπτάδελφοι, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ancient_Greek "Romanization of Ancient Greek"): *Heptádelphoi*;[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Septem Fratres*).[[17]](#cite_note-17) In particular, the Roman stronghold at the site took the name "Fort at the Seven Brothers" (*Castellum ad Septem Fratres*).[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1854-13) This was gradually shortened to Septem[[18]](#cite_note-Kaegi-18) (Σέπτον *Sépton*) or, occasionally, **Septum**[[19]](#cite_note-Kitto-19) or **Septa**.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDyer1873-20) These clipped forms continued as [Berber](/wiki/Berber_language "Berber language") *Sebta* and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") *Sabtan*[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmedley_&_al.184549-15) or *Sabtah* (سبتة‎), which themselves became *Ceuta* in [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") (pronounced [[ˈseu̯tɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") (locally pronounced [[ˈseu̯ta]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Ancient

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ancient")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Catedral_de_la_Asunci%C3%B3n_de_Ceuta_%2811%29.jpg/250px-Catedral_de_la_Asunci%C3%B3n_de_Ceuta_%2811%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Catedral_de_la_Asunci%C3%B3n_de_Ceuta_(11).jpg)

Phoenician archeological site, dated to the 7th century BC, next to the [Cathedral of Ceuta](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Ceuta "Cathedral of Ceuta")

Controlling access between the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") and the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"), the [Strait of Gibraltar](/wiki/Strait_of_Gibraltar "Strait of Gibraltar") is an important military and commercial [chokepoint](/wiki/Chokepoint "Chokepoint"). The [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicians "Phoenicians") realized the extremely narrow isthmus joining the [Peninsula of Almina](/wiki/Peninsula_of_Almina "Peninsula of Almina") to the African mainland made Ceuta eminently defensible and established an outpost there early in the 1st millennium BC. The [Greek geographers](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_geography "Ancient Greek geography") record it by variations of *Abyla*, the ancient name of nearby [Jebel Musa](/wiki/Jebel_Musa_(Morocco) "Jebel Musa (Morocco)"). Beside [Calpe](/wiki/Calpe "Calpe"), the other [Pillar of Hercules](/wiki/Pillars_of_Hercules "Pillars of Hercules") now known as the [Rock of Gibraltar](/wiki/Rock_of_Gibraltar "Rock of Gibraltar"), the Phoenicians established [Kart](/wiki/Carteia "Carteia") at what is now [San Roque](/wiki/San_Roque,_C%C3%A1diz "San Roque, Cádiz"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"). Other good anchorages nearby became [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenician_colonies "Phoenician colonies") and then [Carthaginian](/wiki/Carthaginian_Empire "Carthaginian Empire") ports at what are now [Tangiers](/wiki/Tangiers "Tangiers") and [Cádiz](/wiki/C%C3%A1diz "Cádiz").

After [Carthage](/wiki/Ancient_Carthage "Ancient Carthage")'s [destruction](/wiki/Battle_of_Carthage_(c._149_BC) "Battle of Carthage (c. 149 BC)") in the [Punic Wars](/wiki/Punic_Wars "Punic Wars"), most of [northwest Africa](/wiki/Northwest_Africa "Northwest Africa") was left to the [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") [client states](/wiki/Client_state "Client state") of [Numidia](/wiki/Numidia "Numidia") and—around Abyla—[Mauretania](/wiki/Mauretania "Mauretania"). [Punic culture](/wiki/Punics "Punics") continued to thrive in what the Romans knew as "Septem". After the [Battle of Thapsus](/wiki/Battle_of_Thapsus "Battle of Thapsus") in 46 BC, [Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar") and his heirs began annexing North Africa directly as [Roman provinces](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province") but, as late as [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus"), most of Septem's [Berber](/wiki/Berbers "Berbers") residents continued to speak and write in [Punic](/wiki/Punic_language "Punic language").

[Caligula](/wiki/Caligula "Caligula") assassinated the Mauretanian king [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy_of_Mauretania "Ptolemy of Mauretania") in AD 40 and seized his kingdom, which [Claudius](/wiki/Claudius "Claudius") organized in AD 42, placing Septem in the [province](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province") of [Tingitana](/wiki/Tingitana "Tingitana") and raising it to the level of a [colony](/wiki/Roman_colonia "Roman colonia"). It subsequently was [Romanized](/wiki/Romanization_(cultural) "Romanization (cultural)") and thrived into the late 3rd century, trading heavily with [Roman Spain](/wiki/Hispania "Hispania") and becoming well known for its [salted fish](/wiki/Salted_fish "Salted fish"). [Roads](/wiki/Roman_road "Roman road") connected it overland with [Tingis](/wiki/Tingis "Tingis") (Tangiers) and [Volubilis](/wiki/Volubilis "Volubilis"). Under [Theodosius I](/wiki/Theodosius_I "Theodosius I") in the late 4th century, Septem still had 10,000 inhabitants, nearly all [Christian](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity") [citizens](/wiki/Roman_citizenship "Roman citizenship") speaking [African Romance](/wiki/African_Romance "African Romance"), a local dialect of Latin.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Medieval

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Medieval")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Interior_de_los_Ba%C3%B1os_%C3%81rabes_de_Ceuta.jpg/250px-Interior_de_los_Ba%C3%B1os_%C3%81rabes_de_Ceuta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interior_de_los_Ba%C3%B1os_%C3%81rabes_de_Ceuta.jpg)

The [Arab Baths of Ceuta](/wiki/Arab_Baths_(Ceuta) "Arab Baths (Ceuta)"), built between the 11th and 13th centuries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Murallas_merin%C3%ADes_de_Ceuta.jpg/250px-Murallas_merin%C3%ADes_de_Ceuta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Murallas_merin%C3%ADes_de_Ceuta.jpg)

The [Marinid Walls](/wiki/Marinid_Walls_of_Ceuta "Marinid Walls of Ceuta"), built by order of [Abu Sa'id Uthman II](/wiki/Abu_Sa%27id_Uthman_II "Abu Sa'id Uthman II") in 1328

[Vandals](/wiki/Vandal_Kingdom "Vandal Kingdom"), probably invited by [Count Boniface](/wiki/Bonifacius "Bonifacius") as protection against the empress dowager [Galla Placidia](/wiki/Galla_Placidia "Galla Placidia"), crossed the strait near Tingis around 425 and swiftly overran [Roman North Africa](/wiki/Africa_(Roman_province) "Africa (Roman province)"). Their king, [Gaiseric](/wiki/Gaiseric "Gaiseric"), focused his attention on the rich lands around [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage"); although the Romans eventually accepted his conquests and he continued to raid them anyway, he soon lost control of Tingis and Septem in a series of Berber revolts. When [Justinian](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I") decided to [reconquer the Vandal lands](/wiki/Vandalic_War "Vandalic War"), his victorious general [Belisarius](/wiki/Belisarius "Belisarius") continued along the coast, making Septem a westernmost outpost of the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") around 533. Unlike the former ancient Roman administration, however, Eastern Rome did not push far into the [hinterland](/wiki/Hinterland "Hinterland") and made the more defensible Septem their regional capital in place of Tingis.

[Epidemics](/wiki/Plague_of_Justinian "Plague of Justinian"), less capable successors, and overstretched supply lines forced a retrenchment and left Septem isolated. It is likely that its [count](/wiki/Comes "Comes") (**comes**) was obliged to pay homage to the [Visigoth Kingdom](/wiki/Visigoth_Kingdom "Visigoth Kingdom") in Spain in the early 7th century. There are no reliable contemporary accounts of the end of the [Islamic conquest of the Maghreb](/wiki/Islamic_conquest_of_the_Maghreb "Islamic conquest of the Maghreb") around 710. Instead, the rapid [Muslim conquest of Spain](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Spain "Muslim conquest of Spain") produced [romances](/wiki/Medieval_romance "Medieval romance") concerning [Count Julian](/wiki/Count_Julian "Count Julian") of Septem and his betrayal of Christendom in revenge for the dishonor that befell his daughter at [King Roderick](/wiki/Roderic "Roderic")'s court. Allegedly with Julian's encouragement and instructions, the Berber convert and freedman [Tariq ibn Ziyad](/wiki/Tariq_ibn_Ziyad "Tariq ibn Ziyad") took his garrison from Tangiers across the strait and overran the Spanish so swiftly that both he and his master [Musa bin Nusayr](/wiki/Musa_bin_Nusayr "Musa bin Nusayr") fell afoul of [a jealous caliph](/wiki/Sulayman_ibn_Abd_al-Malik "Sulayman ibn Abd al-Malik"), who stripped them of their wealth and titles.

After the death of Julian, sometimes also described as a king of the [Ghomara Berbers](/wiki/Ghomara_people "Ghomara people"), Berber converts to Islam took direct control of what they called Sebta. It was then destroyed during [their great revolt](/wiki/Berber_Revolt "Berber Revolt") against the [Umayyad Caliphate](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") around 740. Sebta subsequently remained a small village of Muslims and Christians surrounded by ruins until its resettlement in the 9th century by Mâjakas, chief of the Majkasa Berber tribe, who started the short-lived [Banu Isam](/wiki/Banu_Isam "Banu Isam") dynasty.[[22]](#cite_note-GibbKramers1994-22) His great-grandson briefly allied his tribe with the [Idrisids](/wiki/Idrisid "Idrisid"), but Banu Isam rule ended in 931,[[23]](#cite_note-23) when he abdicated in favor of [Abd ar-Rahman III](/wiki/Abd_ar-Rahman_III "Abd ar-Rahman III"), the Umayyad emir of Córdoba who had self-proclaimed as a caliph in 929. Through the overseas conquests of Ceuta in 931 and Melilla in 927 that allowed to enforce direct political and military influence in the fragmented landscape of the north-African coast, crowned by the skillful political subversion resulting in the 944 revolt in eastern Berbery, the power exerted by the Umayyad Caliphate (engaged in struggle against the Fatimids) in the Western Mediterranean took hold.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Chaos ensued with [the collapse of the Caliphate of Córdoba](/wiki/Fitna_of_al-Andalus "Fitna of al-Andalus") in the early 11th century. In the wake of this, the [Banū Hammūd](/wiki/Hammudid_dynasty "Hammudid dynasty") established a petty kingdom (and nominal caliphate) centered in [Málaga](/wiki/M%C3%A1laga "Málaga") and Ceuta—the so-called [Taifa of Málaga](/wiki/Taifa_of_M%C3%A1laga "Taifa of Málaga")—with Málaga as a capital and Ceuta hosting the heir's residence.[[25]](#cite_note-25) In 1056, the link with Málaga was severed as it was conquered by the [Banu Ziri](/wiki/Zirid_dynasty "Zirid dynasty"), while Suqut al-Bargawati remained in power in Ceuta, styling as a caliph after 1061.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalero_Secall199447-26)

Starting in 1084, the [Almoravid](/wiki/Almoravid "Almoravid") Berbers ruled the region until 1147, when the [Almohads](/wiki/Almohad "Almohad") conquered the land. Apart from [Ibn Hud](/wiki/Ibn_Hud "Ibn Hud")'s rebellion in 1232, they ruled until the Tunisian [Hafsids](/wiki/Hafsid "Hafsid") established control. The Hafsids' influence in the west rapidly waned, and Ceuta's inhabitants eventually expelled them in 1249. After this, a period of political instability persisted, under competing interests from the [Marinids](/wiki/Marinids "Marinids") and [Granada](/wiki/Emirate_of_Granada "Emirate of Granada") as well as autonomous rule under the native [Banu al-Azafi](/wiki/Banu_al-Azafi "Banu al-Azafi"). Fez finally conquered the region in 1387, with assistance from [Aragon](/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon").

### Portuguese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Portuguese")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Porto_April_2019-7.jpg/250px-Porto_April_2019-7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Porto_April_2019-7.jpg)

Representation of [Prince Henry the Navigator](/wiki/Henry_the_Navigator "Henry the Navigator") during the [Conquest of Ceuta](/wiki/Conquest_of_Ceuta "Conquest of Ceuta") in [*azulejos*](/wiki/Azulejo "Azulejo") at the [São Bento railway station](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Bento_railway_station "São Bento railway station")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Septa_%28Ceuta%29_ca_1572.jpg/250px-Septa_%28Ceuta%29_ca_1572.jpg)](/wiki/File:Septa_(Ceuta)_ca_1572.jpg)

1572 depiction of Ceuta

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Ceuta_fortifications.jpg/250px-Ceuta_fortifications.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ceuta_fortifications.jpg)

The [Royal Walls of Ceuta](/wiki/Royal_Walls_of_Ceuta "Royal Walls of Ceuta"), built from 962 to the 18th century, and navigable moats

On the morning of 21 August 1415, King [John I of Portugal](/wiki/John_I_of_Portugal "John I of Portugal") led his sons and their assembled forces in a surprise assault that would come to be known as the [Conquest of Ceuta](/wiki/Conquest_of_Ceuta "Conquest of Ceuta"). The 45,000 Portuguese who traveled on 200 ships caught the defenders of Ceuta off guard and suffered only eight casualties. By nightfall the town was captured and on the morning of 22 August, Ceuta was in Portuguese hands. [Álvaro Vaz de Almada, 1st Count of Avranches](/wiki/%C3%81lvaro_Vaz_de_Almada,_1st_Count_of_Avranches "Álvaro Vaz de Almada, 1st Count of Avranches") was asked to hoist what was to become the [flag of Ceuta](/wiki/Flag_of_Ceuta "Flag of Ceuta"), which is identical to the [flag of Lisbon](/wiki/Flag_of_Lisbon "Flag of Lisbon"), but in which the coat of arms derived from that of the [Kingdom of Portugal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal") was added to the center; the original Portuguese flag and [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") of Ceuta remained unchanged, and the modern-day Ceuta flag features the configuration of the [Portuguese shield](/wiki/Flag_of_Portugal#Portuguese_shield "Flag of Portugal").

John's son [Henry the Navigator](/wiki/Henry_the_Navigator "Henry the Navigator") distinguished himself in the battle, being wounded during the conquest. The looting of the city proved to be less profitable than expected for John I, so he decided to keep the city to pursue further enterprises in the area.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

From 1415 to 1437, [Pedro de Meneses](/wiki/Pedro_de_Meneses,_1st_Count_of_Vila_Real "Pedro de Meneses, 1st Count of Vila Real") became the first governor of Ceuta.

The [Marinid Sultanate](/wiki/Marinid_Sultanate "Marinid Sultanate") started the [1419 siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Ceuta_(1419) "Siege of Ceuta (1419)") but was defeated by the first governor of Ceuta before reinforcements arrived in the form of [John, Constable of Portugal](/wiki/John,_Constable_of_Portugal "John, Constable of Portugal") and his brother [Henry the Navigator](/wiki/Henry_the_Navigator "Henry the Navigator"), who were sent with troops to defend Ceuta.

Under [King John I](/wiki/John_I_of_Portugal "John I of Portugal")'s son, [Duarte](/wiki/Edward,_King_of_Portugal "Edward, King of Portugal"), the city of Ceuta rapidly became a drain on the Portuguese treasury. [Trans-Saharan trade](/wiki/Trans-Saharan_trade "Trans-Saharan trade") journeyed instead to [Tangier](/wiki/Tangier "Tangier"). It was soon realized that without the city of Tangier, possession of Ceuta was worthless. In 1437, [Duarte's brothers](/wiki/Illustrious_Generation_(Portugal) "Illustrious Generation (Portugal)") [Henry the Navigator](/wiki/Henry_the_Navigator "Henry the Navigator") and [Fernando, the Saint Prince](/wiki/Fernando,_the_Saint_Prince "Fernando, the Saint Prince") persuaded him to launch an attack on the [Marinid](/wiki/Marinid "Marinid") sultanate. The resulting [Battle of Tangier (1437)](/wiki/Battle_of_Tangier_(1437) "Battle of Tangier (1437)"), led by Henry, was a debacle. In the resulting treaty, Henry promised to deliver Ceuta back to the Marinids in return for allowing the Portuguese army to depart unmolested, which he reneged on.

Possession of Ceuta indirectly led to further [Portuguese expansion](/wiki/History_of_Portugal_(1415%E2%80%931578) "History of Portugal (1415–1578)"). The main area of Portuguese expansion, at this time, was the coast of the [Maghreb](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb"), where there was grain, cattle, sugar, and textiles, as well as fish, hides, wax, and honey.[[28]](#cite_note-Payne-28)

Ceuta had to endure alone for 43 years, until the position of the city was consolidated with the taking of [Ksar es-Seghir](/wiki/Ksar_es-Seghir "Ksar es-Seghir") (1458), [Arzila](/wiki/Arzila "Arzila") and Tangier (1471) by the Portuguese.

The city was recognized as a Portuguese possession by the [Treaty of Alcáçovas](/wiki/Treaty_of_Alc%C3%A1%C3%A7ovas "Treaty of Alcáçovas") (1479) and by the [Treaty of Tordesillas](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tordesillas "Treaty of Tordesillas") (1494).

In the 1540s the Portuguese began building the [Royal Walls of Ceuta](/wiki/Royal_Walls_of_Ceuta "Royal Walls of Ceuta") as they are today including [bastions](/wiki/Bastion "Bastion"), a navigable moat and a drawbridge. Some of these bastions are still standing, like the bastions of Coraza Alta, Bandera and Mallorquines.[[29]](#cite_note-fortified-places-29)

[Luís de Camões](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_de_Cam%C3%B5es "Luís de Camões") lived in Ceuta between 1549 and 1551, losing his right eye in battle, which influenced his work of poetry *[Os Lusíadas](/wiki/Os_Lus%C3%ADadas "Os Lusíadas")*.

### Union between Portugal and Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Union between Portugal and Spain")]

In 1578 King [Sebastian of Portugal](/wiki/Sebastian_of_Portugal "Sebastian of Portugal") died at the [Battle of Alcácer Quibir](/wiki/Battle_of_Alc%C3%A1cer_Quibir "Battle of Alcácer Quibir") (known as the Battle of Three Kings) in what is today northern Morocco, without descendants, triggering the [1580 Portuguese succession crisis](/wiki/1580_Portuguese_succession_crisis "1580 Portuguese succession crisis"). His grand-uncle, the elderly [Cardinal Henry](/wiki/Henry,_King_of_Portugal "Henry, King of Portugal"), succeeded him as King, but also had no descendants, having taken [holy orders](/wiki/Holy_orders "Holy orders"). When the cardinal-king died two years later, three grandchildren of King [Manuel I of Portugal](/wiki/Manuel_I_of_Portugal "Manuel I of Portugal") claimed the throne:

* [Infanta Catarina, Duchess of Braganza](/wiki/Infanta_Catarina,_Duchess_of_Braganza "Infanta Catarina, Duchess of Braganza")
* [António, Prior of Crato](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio,_Prior_of_Crato "António, Prior of Crato")
* [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"), uncle of former King Sebastian of Portugal

Philip prevailed and was crowned King [Philip I of Portugal](/wiki/Philip_I_of_Portugal "Philip I of Portugal") in 1581, [uniting the two crowns and overseas empires](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union").[[30]](#cite_note-Kamen177-30)

During the Union with Spain, 1580 to 1640, Ceuta attracted many residents of Spanish origin[[31]](#cite_note-Griffinh-31) and became the only city of the [Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") that sided with Spain when Portugal regained its independence in the [Portuguese Restoration War](/wiki/Portuguese_Restoration_War "Portuguese Restoration War") of 1640.

### Spanish

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Spanish")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Fort_of_El_Desnarigado.jpg/250px-Fort_of_El_Desnarigado.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fort_of_El_Desnarigado.jpg)

Fort of the Desnarigado, built in the 19th century, houses a museum.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Casa_de_los_Dragones%2C_Ceuta%2C_Espa%C3%B1a%2C_2015-12-10%2C_DD_52.JPG/250px-Casa_de_los_Dragones%2C_Ceuta%2C_Espa%C3%B1a%2C_2015-12-10%2C_DD_52.JPG)](/wiki/File:Casa_de_los_Dragones,_Ceuta,_Espa%C3%B1a,_2015-12-10,_DD_52.JPG)

Eclectic [House of the Dragons](/wiki/Casa_de_los_Dragones "Casa de los Dragones"), built in 1905

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Ceuta_Turn_of_the_century.jpg/250px-Ceuta_Turn_of_the_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ceuta_Turn_of_the_century.jpg)

A street in Ceuta, c. 1905–1910

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Plano-de-la-Ciudad-de-Ceuta-1943.jpg/250px-Plano-de-la-Ciudad-de-Ceuta-1943.jpg)](/wiki/File:Plano-de-la-Ciudad-de-Ceuta-1943.jpg)

Map of Ceuta in the 1940s

On 1 January 1668, King [Afonso VI of Portugal](/wiki/Afonso_VI_of_Portugal "Afonso VI of Portugal") recognised the formal allegiance of Ceuta to Spain and ceded Ceuta to King [Carlos II of Spain](/wiki/Carlos_II_of_Spain "Carlos II of Spain") by the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon_(1668) "Treaty of Lisbon (1668)").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The city was attacked by Moroccan forces under [Moulay Ismail](/wiki/Moulay_Ismail "Moulay Ismail") during the [Siege of Ceuta (1694–1727)](/wiki/Siege_of_Ceuta_(1694%E2%80%931727) "Siege of Ceuta (1694–1727)"). During the longest siege in history, the city underwent changes leading to the loss of its Portuguese character.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] While most of the military operations took place around the [Royal Walls of Ceuta](/wiki/Royal_Walls_of_Ceuta "Royal Walls of Ceuta"), there were also small-scale penetrations by Spanish forces at various points on the Moroccan coast, and seizure of shipping in the Strait of Gibraltar.

During the [Napoleonic Wars (1803–1815)](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), Spain allowed Britain to occupy Ceuta. Occupation began in 1810, with Ceuta being returned at the conclusion of the wars.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Disagreements regarding the border of Ceuta resulted in the [Hispano-Moroccan War (1859–60)](/wiki/Hispano-Moroccan_War_(1859%E2%80%9360) "Hispano-Moroccan War (1859–60)"), which ended at the [Battle of Tetuán](/wiki/Battle_of_T%C3%A9touan "Battle of Tétouan").

In July 1936, General [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco") took command of the [Spanish Army of Africa](/wiki/Spanish_Army_of_Africa "Spanish Army of Africa") and rebelled against the Spanish republican government; his military uprising led to the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") of 1936–1939. Franco transported troops to mainland Spain in an airlift using transport aircraft supplied by [Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"). Ceuta became one of the first battlegrounds of the uprising: General Franco's rebel nationalist forces seized Ceuta, while at the same time the city came under fire from the air and sea forces of the official republican government.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The [Llano Amarillo](/wiki/Monumento_del_Llano_Amarillo "Monumento del Llano Amarillo") monument was erected to honor [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco"); it was inaugurated on 13 July 1940. The tall obelisk has since been abandoned, but the shield symbols of the [Falange](/wiki/Falangism "Falangism") and Imperial Eagle remain visible.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Following the 1947 [Partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India"), a substantial number of [Sindhi Hindus](/wiki/Sindhi_Hindus "Sindhi Hindus") from current-day Pakistan settled in Ceuta, adding to a small Hindu community that had existed in Ceuta since 1893, connected to Gibraltar's.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

When Spain recognized the independence of [Spanish Morocco](/wiki/Spanish_Morocco "Spanish Morocco") in 1956, Ceuta and the other *[plazas de soberanía](/wiki/Plazas_de_soberan%C3%ADa "Plazas de soberanía")* remained under Spanish rule. Spain considered them integral parts of the Spanish state, but Morocco has disputed this point.

Culturally, modern Ceuta is part of the Spanish region of [Andalusia](/wiki/Andalusia "Andalusia"). It was attached to the [province of Cádiz](/wiki/Province_of_C%C3%A1diz "Province of Cádiz") until 1995, the Spanish coast being only 20 km (12.5 miles) away. It is a cosmopolitan city, with a large ethnic [Arab](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs")-[Berber](/wiki/Berbers "Berbers") [Muslim](/wiki/Islam "Islam") minority (although the Berber presence is much less outspoken in Ceuta than in [Melilla](/wiki/Melilla "Melilla"))[[37]](#cite_note-37) as well as [Sephardic](/wiki/Sephardic "Sephardic") Jewish and [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") minorities.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

On 5 November 2007, King [Juan Carlos I](/wiki/Juan_Carlos_I "Juan Carlos I") and Queen [Sofía](/wiki/Queen_Sof%C3%ADa_of_Spain "Queen Sofía of Spain") visited Ceuta and Melilla, sparking enthusiasm from the local population and protests from the Moroccan government, which led to a [brief diplomatic conflict](/wiki/2007_Morocco%E2%80%93Spain_diplomatic_conflict "2007 Morocco–Spain diplomatic conflict").[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) It was the first time a Spanish head of state had visited the two cities since 1927.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Since 2010, Ceuta and Melilla have declared the [Muslim holiday](/wiki/Muslim_holiday "Muslim holiday") of [Eid al-Adha](/wiki/Eid_al-Adha "Eid al-Adha"), or Feast of the Sacrifice, an official public holiday. It is the first time a non-Christian [religious festival](/wiki/Religious_festival "Religious festival") has been officially celebrated in Spanish ruled territory since the [Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista").[[42]](#cite_note-42)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Ceuta_en.png/330px-Ceuta_en.png)](/wiki/File:Ceuta_en.png)

Map of Ceuta (the [Perejil Island](/wiki/Perejil_Island "Perejil Island"), part of Ceuta, is just off the coast, in the upper left of this map)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Strait_of_Gibraltar_perspective.jpg/330px-Strait_of_Gibraltar_perspective.jpg)](/wiki/File:Strait_of_Gibraltar_perspective.jpg)

Perspective view of the [Strait of Gibraltar](/wiki/Strait_of_Gibraltar "Strait of Gibraltar") facing eastwards; Spain and [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") on the left; Morocco and Ceuta on the right. The vertical dimension is exaggerated by a factor of 3.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Ceuta2022OSM.png/500px-Ceuta2022OSM.png)](/wiki/File:Ceuta2022OSM.png)

A highly detailed map of Ceuta

Ceuta is separated by 17 km (11 mi)[[44]](#cite_note-44) from the [province of Cádiz](/wiki/Province_of_C%C3%A1diz "Province of Cádiz") on the Spanish mainland by the [Strait of Gibraltar](/wiki/Strait_of_Gibraltar "Strait of Gibraltar") and it shares a 6.4 km (4 mi) land [border](/wiki/Morocco%E2%80%93Spain_border "Morocco–Spain border") with [M'diq-Fnideq Prefecture](/wiki/M%27diq-Fnideq_Prefecture "M'diq-Fnideq Prefecture") in the [Kingdom of Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"). It has an area of 18.5 km2 (7 sq mi; 4,571 acres). It is dominated by Monte Anyera, a hill along its western frontier with Morocco, which is guarded by a Spanish [military](/wiki/Spanish_army "Spanish army") fort. [Monte Hacho](/wiki/Monte_Hacho "Monte Hacho") on the [Peninsula of Almina](/wiki/Peninsula_of_Almina "Peninsula of Almina") overlooking the [port](/wiki/Port_of_Ceuta "Port of Ceuta") is one of the possible locations of the southern pillar of the [Pillars of Hercules](/wiki/Pillars_of_Hercules "Pillars of Hercules") of Greek legend (the other possibility being [Jebel Musa](/wiki/Jebel_Musa_(Morocco) "Jebel Musa (Morocco)")).[[45]](#cite_note-TarverSlape2016-45)

### Important Bird Area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Important Bird Area")]

The Ceuta Peninsula has been recognised as an [Important Bird Area](/wiki/Important_Bird_Area "Important Bird Area") (IBA) by [BirdLife International](/wiki/BirdLife_International "BirdLife International") because the site is part of a migratory bottleneck, or choke point, at the western end of the Mediterranean for large numbers of [raptors](/wiki/Bird_of_prey "Bird of prey"), [storks](/wiki/Stork "Stork") and other birds flying between Europe and Africa. These include [European honey buzzards](/wiki/European_honey_buzzard "European honey buzzard"), [black kites](/wiki/Black_kite "Black kite"), [short-toed snake eagles](/wiki/Short-toed_snake_eagle "Short-toed snake eagle"), [Egyptian vultures](/wiki/Egyptian_vulture "Egyptian vulture"), [griffon vultures](/wiki/Griffon_vulture "Griffon vulture"), [black storks](/wiki/Black_stork "Black stork"), [white storks](/wiki/White_stork "White stork") and [Audouin's gulls](/wiki/Audouin%27s_gull "Audouin's gull").[[46]](#cite_note-bli-46)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

Ceuta has a maritime-influenced [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate"), similar to nearby Spanish and Moroccan cities such as [Tarifa](/wiki/Tarifa "Tarifa"), [Algeciras](/wiki/Algeciras "Algeciras") or [Tangiers](/wiki/Tangiers "Tangiers").[[47]](#cite_note-47) The average [diurnal temperature variation](/wiki/Diurnal_temperature_variation "Diurnal temperature variation") is relatively low; the average annual temperature is 18.8 °C (65.8 °F) with average yearly highs of 21.4 °C (70.5 °F) and lows of 15.7 °C (60.3 °F) though the Ceuta weather station has only been in operation since 2003.[[48]](#cite_note-Standard_climate_values_for_Ceuta-48) Ceuta has relatively mild winters for the latitude, while summers are warm yet milder than in the interior of Southern Spain, due to the moderating effect of the Straits of Gibraltar. Summers are very dry, but yearly precipitation is still at 849 mm (33.4 in),[[48]](#cite_note-Standard_climate_values_for_Ceuta-48) which could be considered a humid climate if the summers were not so arid.

## Government and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Government and administration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Palacio_de_la_Asamblea_de_Ceuta.jpg/250px-Palacio_de_la_Asamblea_de_Ceuta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palacio_de_la_Asamblea_de_Ceuta.jpg)

The Palacio de la Asamblea de Ceuta is the seat of the [Assembly of Ceuta](/wiki/Assembly_of_Ceuta "Assembly of Ceuta").

Since 1995, Ceuta is, along with [Melilla](/wiki/Melilla "Melilla"), one of the two [autonomous cities](/wiki/Autonomous_community#autonomous_cities "Autonomous community") of Spain.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Ceuta is known officially in Spanish as *Ciudad Autónoma de Ceuta* (English: *Autonomous City of Ceuta*), with a rank between a standard [municipality](/wiki/Municipalities_in_Spain "Municipalities in Spain") and an [autonomous community](/wiki/Autonomous_communities_of_Spain "Autonomous communities of Spain"). Ceuta is part of the territory of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). The city was a [free port](/wiki/Free_port "Free port") before Spain joined the European Union in 1986. Now it has a low-tax system within the [Economic and Monetary Union of the European Union](/wiki/Economic_and_Monetary_Union_of_the_European_Union "Economic and Monetary Union of the European Union").

Since 1979, Ceuta has held elections to its 25-seat [assembly](/wiki/Assembly_of_Ceuta "Assembly of Ceuta") every four years. The leader of its government was the Mayor until the Autonomy Statute provided for the new title of [Mayor-President](/wiki/Mayor-President "Mayor-President"). As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit), the [People's Party](/wiki/People%27s_Party_(Spain) "People's Party (Spain)") (PP) won 18 seats, keeping [Juan Jesús Vivas](/wiki/Juan_Jes%C3%BAs_Vivas "Juan Jesús Vivas") as Mayor-President, which he has been since 2001. The remaining seats are held by the regionalist [Caballas Coalition](/wiki/Caballas_Coalition "Caballas Coalition") (4) and the [Socialist Workers' Party](/wiki/Socialist_Workers%27_Party_(Spain) "Socialist Workers' Party (Spain)") (PSOE, 3).[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Owing to its small population, Ceuta elects only one member of the [Congress of Deputies](/wiki/Congress_of_Deputies_(Spain) "Congress of Deputies (Spain)"), the lower house of the *[Cortes Generales](/wiki/Cortes_Generales "Cortes Generales")* (the Spanish Parliament). As of the November 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit) election, this post is held by María Teresa López of [Vox](/wiki/Vox_(political_party) "Vox (political party)").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Ceuta is subdivided into 63 *barriadas* ("neighborhoods"), such as Barriada de Berizu, Barriada de P. Alfonso, Barriada del Sarchal, and El Hacho.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Ceuta maintains its own police force.

## Defence and Civil Guard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Defence and Civil Guard")]

Defence of the territory is the responsibility of the [Spanish Armed Forces](/wiki/Spanish_Armed_Forces "Spanish Armed Forces")' General Command of Ceuta (COMGECEU).[[56]](#cite_note-Contenido_-_Army-56) The [Spanish Army](/wiki/Spanish_Army "Spanish Army")'s combat components of the command include:

* [54th *Regulares* Infantry Regiment](/wiki/Regulares "Regulares") based in *González Tablas* barracks;
* [2nd Tercio *Duke of Alba* Regiment](/wiki/Tercio_%22Duque_de_Alba%22_No._2_of_the_Legion "Tercio \"Duque de Alba\" No. 2 of the Legion") of the [Spanish Legion](/wiki/Spanish_Legion "Spanish Legion") based in the Seraglio-Recarga cantonment;
* 3rd "Montesa" Cavalry Regiment (RC-3) located in the *Colonel Galindo* barracks and equipped with [Leopard 2 main battle tanks](/wiki/Leopard_2 "Leopard 2") and [Pizarro infantry fighting vehicles](/wiki/ASCOD "ASCOD")[[57]](#cite_note-57)
* 30th Mixed Artillery Regiment, one group equipped with [155/52mm towed howitzers](/wiki/Santa_B%C3%A1rbara_Sistemas_155/52 "Santa Bárbara Sistemas 155/52") and the other with [Mistral short-range SAMs](/wiki/Mistral_(missile) "Mistral (missile)") and [35/90 SKYDOR/35/90 GDF-007 anti-aircraft guns](/wiki/Oerlikon_GDF "Oerlikon GDF") fulfilling an air defence role;[[58]](#cite_note-58) and
* 7th Engineer Regiment

The command also includes its headquarters battalion as well as logistics elements.[[56]](#cite_note-Contenido_-_Army-56)

In 2023, the [Spanish Navy](/wiki/Spanish_Navy "Spanish Navy") replaced the *Aresa*-class patrol boat *P-114* in the territory with the *Rodman*-class patrol boat *Isla de León*.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Ceuta itself is only 113 km (70 mi) distant from the main Spanish naval base at [Rota](/wiki/Naval_Station_Rota "Naval Station Rota") on the Spanish mainland. The [Spanish Air Force](/wiki/Spanish_Air_Force "Spanish Air Force")'s [Morón Air Base](/wiki/Mor%C3%B3n_Air_Base "Morón Air Base") is also within 135 km (84 mi) proximity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Civil Guard](/wiki/Civil_Guard_(Spain) "Civil Guard (Spain)") is responsible for border security and protects both the territory's [fortified land border](/wiki/Ceuta_border_fence "Ceuta border fence") as well as its maritime approaches against frequent, and sometimes significant, migrant incursions.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Jebel_musa_from_benzu.jpg/250px-Jebel_musa_from_benzu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jebel_musa_from_benzu.jpg)

The Moroccan mountain of [Jebel Musa](/wiki/Jebel_Musa,_Morocco "Jebel Musa, Morocco"), as viewed from [Benzú](/wiki/Benz%C3%BA "Benzú"). It is also known as the 'Dead Woman' because of its silhouette.

The official currency of Ceuta is the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro"). It is part of a special low tax zone in Spain.[[61]](#cite_note-Ceuta.es-61) Ceuta is one of two Spanish port cities on the northern shore of Africa, along with [Melilla](/wiki/Melilla "Melilla"). They are historically military strongholds, [free ports](/wiki/Free_port "Free port"), oil ports, and also fishing ports.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Today the economy of the city depends heavily on its port (now in expansion) and its industrial and retail centres.[[61]](#cite_note-Ceuta.es-61) [Ceuta Heliport](/wiki/Ceuta_Heliport "Ceuta Heliport") is now used to connect the city to mainland Spain by air. [Lidl](/wiki/Lidl "Lidl"), [Decathlon](/wiki/Decathlon_(retailer) "Decathlon (retailer)") and [El Corte Inglés](/wiki/El_Corte_Ingl%C3%A9s "El Corte Inglés") have branches in Ceuta. There is also a [casino](/wiki/Casino "Casino").
Border trade between Ceuta and Morocco is active because of advantage of tax-free status. Thousands of Moroccan women are involved in the cross-border porter trade daily, as [porteadoras](/wiki/Porteadoras "Porteadoras"). The [Moroccan dirham](/wiki/Moroccan_dirham "Moroccan dirham") is used in such trade, even though prices are marked in euros.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Transport")]

The city's [Port of Ceuta](/wiki/Port_of_Ceuta "Port of Ceuta") is connected to the [Port of Algeciras](/wiki/Port_of_Algeciras "Port of Algeciras") across the Strait of Gibraltar by multiple daily sailings of ferries.

A single road border checkpoint to the south of Ceuta near Fnideq allows for cars and pedestrians to travel between Morocco and Spain. An additional border crossing for pedestrians exists between Benzú and [Belyounech](/wiki/Belyounech "Belyounech") on the northern coast. The rest of the border is closed and inaccessible.

There is a bus service throughout the city, and while it does not pass into neighbouring Morocco, it services both frontier crossings.

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Restos_de_la_Bas%C3%ADlica_Tardorromana_de_Ceuta.jpg/250px-Restos_de_la_Bas%C3%ADlica_Tardorromana_de_Ceuta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Restos_de_la_Bas%C3%ADlica_Tardorromana_de_Ceuta.jpg)

Remains of the Late Roman Christian Basilica and Necropolis of Ceuta, dated to the mid-4th century AD or the beginning of the 5th century AD

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Catedral_de_Ceuta%2C_Ceuta%2C_Espa%C3%B1a%2C_2015-12-10%2C_DD_04.JPG/250px-Catedral_de_Ceuta%2C_Ceuta%2C_Espa%C3%B1a%2C_2015-12-10%2C_DD_04.JPG)](/wiki/File:Catedral_de_Ceuta,_Ceuta,_Espa%C3%B1a,_2015-12-10,_DD_04.JPG)

[Cathedral of St. Mary of the Assumption](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St_Mary_of_the_Assumption_(Ceuta) "Cathedral of St Mary of the Assumption (Ceuta)"), completed in 1726

[Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") has been present in Ceuta continuously from [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity"), as evidenced by the ruins of a basilica in downtown Ceuta[[77]](#cite_note-77) and accounts of the [martyrdom](/wiki/Christian_martyrdom "Christian martyrdom") of [St. Daniel Fasanella](/wiki/Daniel_and_companions "Daniel and companions") and his [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscan_Order "Franciscan Order") in 1227 during the [Almohad Caliphate](/wiki/Almohad_Caliphate "Almohad Caliphate").

The town's Grand Mosque had been built over a [Byzantine-era](/wiki/Byzantine_architecture "Byzantine architecture") church. In 1415, the year of the city's conquest, the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") converted the Grand Mosque into [Ceuta Cathedral](/wiki/Ceuta_Cathedral "Ceuta Cathedral"). The present form of the cathedral dates to refurbishments undertaken in the late 17th century, combining [baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") and [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") elements. It was [dedicated](/wiki/Dedication_(ritual) "Dedication (ritual)") to [St Mary of the Assumption](/wiki/Saint_Mary_of_the_Assumption "Saint Mary of the Assumption") in 1726.

The [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") [Diocese of Ceuta](/wiki/Diocese_of_Ceuta "Diocese of Ceuta") was established in 1417. It incorporated the suppressed [Diocese of Tanger](/wiki/Diocese_of_Tanger "Diocese of Tanger") in 1570.[[78]](#cite_note-78) The Diocese of Ceuta was a [suffragan](/wiki/Suffragan "Suffragan") of [Lisbon](/wiki/Patriarchate_of_Lisbon "Patriarchate of Lisbon") until 1675, when it became a suffragan of [Seville](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Seville "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Seville").[[79]](#cite_note-79) In 1851, Ceuta's administration was notionally merged into the [Diocese of Cádiz and Ceuta](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Cadiz_y_Ceuta "Roman Catholic Diocese of Cadiz y Ceuta") as part of [a concordat](/wiki/Concordat_of_1851 "Concordat of 1851") between Spain and the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See");[[80]](#cite_note-80) the union was not actually accomplished, however, until 1879.

Small [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") and [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") minorities are also present in the city.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

### Migration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Migration")]

Main article: [Ceuta border fence](/wiki/Ceuta_border_fence "Ceuta border fence")

Like [Melilla](/wiki/Melilla#Immigration "Melilla"), Ceuta attracts African migrants who try to use it as an entry to Europe. As a result, the territory is surrounded by double fences that are 6 m (20 ft) high, and hundreds of migrants congregate near the fences waiting for a chance to cross them. The fences are regularly stormed by migrants trying to claim asylum once they enter Ceuta.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Education")]

The [University of Granada](/wiki/University_of_Granada#Campus_located_in_Ceuta "University of Granada") offers undergraduate programmes at their campus in Ceuta. Like all areas of Spain, Ceuta is also served by the [National University of Distance Education (UNED)](/wiki/National_University_of_Distance_Education "National University of Distance Education").

While primary and secondary education are generally offered in Spanish only, a growing number of schools are entering the Bilingual Education Programme.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

## Dispute with Morocco

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ceuta&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Dispute with Morocco")]

See also: [Greater Morocco](/wiki/Greater_Morocco "Greater Morocco")

The [Moroccan government](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Morocco "Cabinet of Morocco") has repeatedly called for Spain to transfer the sovereignty of Ceuta, [Melilla](/wiki/Melilla "Melilla") and the *[plazas de soberanía](/wiki/Plazas_de_soberan%C3%ADa "Plazas de soberanía")* to Morocco, with Spain's refusal to do so serving as a major source of tension in [Morocco–Spain relations](/wiki/Morocco%E2%80%93Spain_relations "Morocco–Spain relations"). In Morocco, Ceuta is frequently referred to as the "[occupied](/wiki/Military_occupation "Military occupation") Sebtah", and the Moroccan government has argued that the city, along with other Spanish territories in the region, are [colonies](/wiki/Colony "Colony").[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) One of the major arguments used by Morocco in their attempts to acquire sovereignty over Ceuta refers to the geographical position of the city, as Ceuta is an [exclave](/wiki/Enclave_and_exclave "Enclave and exclave") surrounded by Moroccan territory and the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") and has no territorial continuity with the rest of Spain.[[95]](#cite_note-95) This argument was originally developed by one of the founders of the Moroccan [Istiqlal Party](/wiki/Istiqlal_Party "Istiqlal Party"), Alal-El Faasi, who openly advocated for Morocco to invade and occupy Ceuta and other North African territories under Spanish rule.[[96]](#cite_note-96) Spain, in line with the majority of nations in the rest of the world, has never recognized Morocco's claim over Ceuta. The official position of the [Spanish government](/wiki/Government_of_Spain "Government of Spain") is that Ceuta is an integral part of Spain, and has been since the 16th century, centuries prior to Morocco's independence from Spain and France in 1956.[[97]](#cite_note-97) The majority of Ceuta's population support continued Spanish sovereignty and are opposed to Moroccan control over the territory.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

In 1986, Spain joined [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"). However, Ceuta is not under NATO protection since Article 6 of the [North Atlantic Treaty](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Treaty "North Atlantic Treaty") limits such coverage to Europe and North America and islands north of the [Tropic of Cancer](/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer "Tropic of Cancer"). However, [French Algeria](/wiki/French_Algeria "French Algeria") was explicitly included in the treaty upon France's entry. Legal experts have claimed that other articles of the treaty could cover Spanish territories in North Africa but this interpretation has not been tested in practice.[[99]](#cite_note-Newtral-99) During the [2022 Madrid summit](/wiki/2022_Madrid_summit "2022 Madrid summit"), the issue of the protection of Ceuta was raised by Spain, with NATO Secretary General [Jens Stoltenberg](/wiki/Jens_Stoltenberg "Jens Stoltenberg") stating: "On which territories NATO protects and Ceuta and Melilla, NATO is there to protect all Allies against any threats. At the end of the day, it will always be a political decision to invoke Article 5, but rest assured NATO is there to protect and defend all Allies".[[100]](#cite_note-100) On 21 December 2020, following statements made by Moroccan Prime Minister [Saadeddine Othmani](/wiki/Saadeddine_Othmani "Saadeddine Othmani") that Ceuta is "Moroccan as the [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara "Sahara")", the Spanish government summoned the Moroccan ambassador, Karima Benyaich, to convey that Spain expects all its partners to respect the sovereignty and territorial integrity of its territory in Africa and asked for an explanation for Othmani's words.[[101]](#cite_note-101)
